speaker: interviewer
text: Please describe the city that you grew up in.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Dusseldorf was a industrial city. And it had an old section and a new section. I don't-- I remember where we lived, but I can't tell you where. It was probably the newer section. It was a big apartment house. It wasn't very tall. In those days, they didn't build them very tall. But it was a very, very large apartment. It was huge. Because my parents entertained a lot. And they had, you know, people come in and have-- oh, yeah. I had to learn the piano, because I had to perform when somebody was there and come out and learn the latest thing. I hated it. [LAUGHS] I was very, very shy. I never wanted to be involved in that, but that's what you had to do. And even my piano was not-- I was not that crazy about it. I don't think I was very good at it. I-- I didn't show tremendous talent. Let me put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, the city, as I said, was industrial-- was a beautiful city. I went back there just once when I had a couple of days. And I remembered-- I found-- even found my school. It was surprising that I found my school. And they had beautiful parks.

speaker: interviewee
text: In other words, where my father's store was, it was called Koenig's Alley, which means the King's Alley. In the middle of the street was a river. It was just beautiful. And it had, at the end, it had waterfall with-- with the monument of something or other-- you know-- that spewed the water out. It was a beautiful, beautiful. It was the most fantastic street in Dusseldorf. It was-- there were a lot of stores there. They're still there. I found my father's store. The stores in there-- well, of course, there was no fur store. It was a leather store, I think. But it was there. And I remember it. I would find it again today. It was. And that was the street where we lived. And they had a lot of parks-- very beautiful parks. And I sat-- the Rhine-- the river Rhine. We also-- the river Rhine also had a-- a section that was called the beach. You could go swimming in the Rhine. You can't do that today. It's totally polluted. I found out that you can't even put your hand in there. But it was wonderful then. There was a-- there was a beach. We went there in the summer, and I swam. And it was quite a thing, because the stream went, you know, one way. So, you had to either go with the stream or try to go against the stream, which was very difficult. And we spent a lot of time there, I remember, in the summer when it was hot. It was beautiful. I think I have one picture of that with my cousin. My cousin, Elsa-- well, I admired her, because she was a teenager, and I was just a child. And she had beautiful clothes. And I watched her put her clothes on, and go out, and you know, and have dates and try to find out what she was doing when-- [LAUGHS] --of course she didn't tell me too much. But I admired her. And I wanted-- she was-- I always thought she was beautiful. And I have a picture of her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any special places that you remember in the city that your-- your parents or your cousin visited, or you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the river Rhine. I mean, we used to go on the boat trips quite often with other-- with other couples and children. So, I remember I always had people-- kids to play with. And we used to go down the river Rhine and stop at one of these places and have a picnic. And it was just a wonderful day. You just went for the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family belong to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we belonged to a synagogue. And we went-- always went on holidays. My father was, as I said, my parents decided not to be orthodox. So they didn't go on Saturdays or Friday nights, except on the holidays. We've preserved all the holidays-- Passover and, of course, Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur. My parents fasted on Yom Kippur. That was the only time he closed the store. Oh, and then Rosh Hashanah he closed the store on then. But he was open on Saturday mornings. Nobody was open after noon anyway, in those days. Saturday was a 1/2 a day, and so he was open on Saturday mornings. And he found that was important.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, we were definitely brought up Jewish and learned all of the-- about the holidays, and what it was all about, and all of the history, and all of this in the-- in the Jewish school. But my mother often said she's-- she couldn't stand the way she was brought up. And my father said the same thing, so they both agreed on that. That was very good for us, I thought. Were there a lot of Jewish people in the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there were. They were scattered. And I had some-- some kids that my parents knew that were Jewish. But most of my friends were from school. They were not Jewish. And that was unfortunate. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe your friends at school and your school life.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- well, as I said, I went to an all girls school. So there were all girls. There were no boys there. And we used to go to each other's birthday party, and go to each other's houses, and do homework together, and maybe go swimming together. Or I invited-- we invited them to come to us on a Sunday and go down, you know, to river Rhine or go someplace else. I always had somebody that I could play with. I don't remember one particular friend except somebody in school who, after Hitler came to power, spit on me. And one of them kicked me down the stairs. And that was the biggest shock to me. And everything was fine until January the 30th 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was when Hitler was appointed. He was never elected. I mean, to this day, people said he was elected. But when he was elected, it was not a real election, 'cause the Jews couldn't vote, the unionists couldn't vote, these people couldn't vote. So, where was the election? It wasn't really a democratic election. So, when you read that he was elected, he was not elected. He was appointed by Hindenburg. And-- and-- and before that, I knew a little bit something. The Nazis started a party, because in-- in the '20s, I understand, he tried to take over Germany in Munich. And this is what he called the putsch. And he was arrested. And he was in jail for a year for trying to start-- Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: Hitler was Austrian. He was born in Austria. And he was-- I don't know whether you read his history-- he tried to be an artist, and he couldn't. He went to Vienna and tried to get into one of the art schools, and they wouldn't have him. And he hadn't forgotten that. And he claimed it was the Jews, of course. Where he got his hatred of Jews, to this day, nobody really knows. Because I don't think he knew many Jews. He grew up-- he was Catholic. Even though today they say that in his background there were Jews, but I-- nobody really knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, he was brought up Catholic, only he was-- and he was in the army-- in the same army my father was in. And when he came out, he was not even sergeant. I think he was just nothing-- just a soldier. He wanted to be-- go into one of the-- the higher place art schools in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I want you to understand something about Vienna. It's a very snobbish city. Unless you knew somebody or-- or had tremendous talent, that's-- and even when-- if you had tremendous talent, you have to have somebody that would recommend you, or you had to come from a family that they would accept. You couldn't get into any of those schools. They were all private schools. There was no such thing as a government supported school. I mean, the-- the government schools-- the-- you had to go to school, I understand, until you were 14 or 15. You didn't have to go any further. But that was the public schools. And they weren't very good. So all the Jewish families I knew, all their children went to private schools-- to higher education schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in Germany, it was understood that once I went-- came out of that school, I would go to a college. Where-- and then my mother had an idea that maybe she would send me to Switzerland for a year to learn some--

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] --or something. There was a school in Switzerland for girls. I wasn't too crazy about that kind of a school. But she was talking about it. And I wasn't-- it wasn't, you know, even-- I wasn't old enough so, we dropped it. But-- but I remember one thing I-- I kept on saying. When my cousin came from Romania, and they were-- I remember her vaguely. I don't remember her name. But they were looking for a husband for her, and that's what they did. They would find Jewish young men, and they had to be either professionals, like a lawyer, or dentist, or doctor, or he had to have a business. And then they would introduce these two people. They had coffee and cake in the house and invite the young man. This is what they did. And then they could go out on a date, which was very formal. They used to go to a theatre or something. And then they had to come right back, you know. I mean, there was no such thing as dating that the parents didn't know the young man. There was no such thing. Except my-- my-- I think my-- my cousin Elsa dated people that weren't recommended. But my father was kind of, sort of relaxed about that. She didn't get along with her father at all. So, her-- she didn't listen to her father at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, she went out with someone, but he had to come to the house first. Regardless of where she met him, he had to come to the house, and introduce himself, and say where he lived, and who his parents were. That was the thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I remember this woman came, and she was already 18. And they said-- she was 19, or she was 20. She will have to be married by before she was 20-- that was the thing. Because I don't know why, but that was the thing. And she-- and I remember we had a young men coming. And she didn't like this one, and this one was too fat, and this one's nose she didn't like. And this one's ears she didn't like, and this one's hair she didn't like-- until they found her somebody she could go out with. I think she married somebody that became a doctor or something-- I remember. But they went back to Romania. But they were looking for her. That's how it was done. And I remember this whole fuss. And I said to my mother, you know, I'm not going to get married-- ever. [LAUGHS] And she says, wait till you get older. You'll change your mind. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I'm not going to go through this. Let's go back. You said when Hitler was appointed in '33-- did you feel any anti-Semitism before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediate-- no, no. I didn't even know what that was. I had-- in-- in Dusseldorf, we lived a perfectly normal life. We had-- my parents had a lot of friends that were not-- that were Catholic. As a matter of fact, I think my parents had a friend, a woman, who fell in love with a priest. And he left the priesthood. And they came to our house. So, they had a lot of friends that were not Jewish. We had a mixture of Jewish and non-Jewish friends. And nobody ever-- I just knew it-- but it wasn't only in that particular case. But nobody ever said no, these are Jewish and these are not Jewish. I never even heard the word anti-Semitism. I didn't know what that meant. The-- the hatred of Jews-- I mean, that-- only Hitler brought that in. There were some things before he came to power, of course. But I was a child. I wasn't that much interested in politics. I heard about the nationalists, the Nazi party.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- it was called the [GERMAN] uh, help me out there. What was it called? Christopher? No. He can't say that. The [GERMAN] party-- or [GERMAN]. It was social and Nazi, you know. And they were called Nazis.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they had the swastika, which is called a [GERMAN] in-- in German. A [GERMAN] is a cross. And it had these little things on there, that's the [GERMAN]. And so it was called this [GERMAN]. And I vaguely heard about the putsch in Munich. But-- and then he wrote his in jail. But I never read it until after he came to power-- much later. I only read parts of it. It was all ugly. I couldn't read it. And it was very rarely that I gave a book that I couldn't read, but his book I couldn't read. And I heard about it. And there were some of the-- they tried to bring out a paper with hatred of Jews. And everybody said, oh, that's ridiculous. The people in the-- in the highest echelons, some of whom were Jewish-- I mean, professors were Jewish. And there was Sigmund Freud. And in the theater, 1/2 the people were Jewish! So, they-- they thought it was ridiculous that they would even talk about it. But he was appointed. And he immediately-- immediately a newspaper came out, which was one. And it had these ugly, ugly pictures of Jews that were going after young girls or something like that-- really ugly stories that were totally ridiculous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you read it yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I read it myself. You couldn't help but-- had it around. I mean, it didn't come into the house, but it was around. You could always--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it in the schools or was it in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah, people would bring it into schools. And even though we-- I was not-- I was not even 12 yet. I was 11 and a 1/2. But the next day, my very good friend spit on me, and said, you dirty Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to her, what did I do? You know? You-- you're a dirty Jew.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remember there was some kids that tried to push me down the stairs. I had my books in my hands, and they pushed the books out of my hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I started to cry. And I said, I didn't do anything. What do you want from me? And as a matter of fact, I went, and I looked in the mirror. I wanted to see. Did I change? Did I become something I wasn't yesterday? What did I do? I mean, then we-- of course, we had that big, big day in the [NON-ENGLISH]. We were told that all the kids had to come to a-- a big talk by the director. And that's in [NON-ENGLISH]?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the [NON-ENGLISH]. Today it would be a gymnasium. It would be probably the-- where everybody in the whole school would be there-- all ages. I was in the lower. You know, I started at 10, so I was 11 and-- I was 11 and a 1/2. So-- and I wasn't quite 10 when I started, so it was-- so, I was 11 and a 1/2. And when we-- we all got in there. And we all had our rooms. You know, each room had their section. And I was in a section. And-- and there was the stage. And there was the director. And on each side were the Nazis with the band-aids and the two Nazis flags on each side. The Austrian flag wasn't there. It was just the Nazi flags-- I mean the German flag-- the old German flag wasn't there. That was the Nazi. Only the Nazi flags were there. And the director got up. And I want you to know-- I mean, the only way you knew the director-- if you did something, you were sent to his office. And that was like death. Everybody was really scared to death of that. I mean, other than that, he doesn't even know you. He didn't know anybody. He was like a god.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he got up. And he-- I never forgot that. I-- to this day, I can see him there. And he was sort of getting hoarse and started to talk. And he started to get up. And he was saying he wanted to talk about the new Germany. And Hitler is now our furor, and that we had to listen to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the reason that-- and he talked about the Versailles Treaty, after the first World War. I don't know whether you're aware of that, but I became aware of that. And I learned about it from that day on. Because then I learned about it-- that after Germany lost the war then, the allies-- the British, and American, and French-- they made them pay a lot of-- a lot of money. And they had to give them a lot of cattle, and-- and-- and-- farming supplies, and I don't know. Anyway, they made Germany pay dearly for losing the war. In other words, it wasn't enough that they lost the war. They punished them on top of that. And that he said, the Versailles Treaty is now no good. He has just finished that. We're not going to pay any more. And we are going to be a big country again. And of course, the whole thing was the-- was the reason. The reason we had that was the Jews. And I couldn't understand that. [LAUGHS] I had never even heard that before. It was all the fault of the Jews. And I looked around. There were very few Jewish girls in that-- in that whole thing. And then after he was through, he said, he will now-- we're going to have a different kind of a country. And the Jews will pay dearly for what they did to Germany. And I couldn't understand what they did to Germany. My father was in a war. He was-- he had a medal or two, I don't remember. I know he had one. And he was wounded twice. He was in the hospital twice, very badly wounded. He lost his hair to some-- he was bald, as you can see. But he lost his hair, he said, in the-- he had some sort of a disease he got in those days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was in the war for-- for either three or four years as a young man. And he did his duty. What-- what did he do? What did he do? He didn't make up the Versailles Treaty.

speaker: interviewee
text: And also the other people that he knew-- I-- that I knew were Jewish-- they were all in the war. All of my-- my Jewish friends' fathers had all been in the war. There was no such thing as getting out of it in those days. You went into the war. And I mean, this was-- so then, when he said that, you know, you looked around. And you wanted to-- I remember forever I wanted the earth to open up, and I wanted to fall in there. That's-- and then he said, we're all going to sing the national anthem. But the Jews are not to stand up and sing, because they're no longer Germans. And I looked around. They were very few, really. Considering it was a large school, there weren't that many that sat-- had to sit. Afterwards, when I-- I was shaking. I was shaking when I came out of there. And then we were sent home. And I came home crying. And I said, they said I was-- I was responsible for-- [LAUGHS] --what happened to Germany. I didn't even know what he was talking about. I didn't know about unemployment and stuff like that. I was a child. That wasn't on my-- it wasn't the things I read. When I read the paper, I read about the movie stars coming and going, and this sort of thing. I was a real child. I was involved in things like that. And then, I remembered all the books I read. And I said, well, that's going to come over in the books. It always comes out good. You know, the bad people will always-- will always pay for what they're doing. But then, my father said this is not-- we have to-- probably we'll have to leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, just about I think a month later-- thus, his business burned down. It was-- obviously it was set on fire. Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see it until it was over. I wasn't allowed there. You know, we were very restricted as children. We were not to do-- we were not to learn things that adults did. We were not to learn the facts of life. At that time, I thought you had to be married to have a baby. [LAUGHS] I mean, when I heard of some girl having a baby, I was shocked. I couldn't understand how she could have a baby. She wasn't married. That's how naive I was. I didn't even know anything. And that's the way, at least the people that I knew, were brought up. And if I learned anything, I went to the library. And I learned from books. I didn't learn from my mother. She never explained anything to me. I don't know when she was going to explain that to me-- maybe just before I got married or something like that. I don't understand. She never really talked to me like mother-daughter. We didn't have that kind of relationship. I-- I loved my father. I was crazy about my father. My papa, he was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- then, my father took all his-- whatever was left over. And he opened up another store in a not so nice area. I remember then. And I don't think he did very well, because then they started saying, don't buy from Jews. They all cheat you, and stuff like that. And it started getting very bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was-- and-- yeah, I got a letter from the school saying, this is now an Aryan school and Jewish people are not allowed there. So, I was kicked out of school. But the one thing I did, my-- my teacher, he must have known. Because he took me aside. And he said, I will see to it that you get your grades and a certificate. Because without that, I couldn't have gone to any school. You needed something-- to this day, you needed paperwork. And he saw to it that I got it. And I-- he did send it to me. Because if I had been kicked out, I couldn't have-- no school would have accepted me. Because there was none-- no paperwork. You could say you went to school, but it didn't mean anything unless you had the proof. So-- so I was not in school and couldn't go anyplace. And I had no friends. Most of my friends were not Jewish. And they weren't talking to me. So I was very unhappy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, they didn't know what to do with me. So, it was decided that I should go to Vienna by myself to my Uncle Artur, my mother's brother, her younger brother, Artur. He was a dentist. And he lived in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that maybe we would go-- my grandmother, of course, said we have to go back to Vienna. We have to go back to Vienna. And my father said, you know, that's the worst thing we could do. Because he's going to come to-- he's going to take over Austria. And everybody said, oh don't be ridiculous. That's ridiculous. He isn't even going to last here. And you don't want to stay here. And then we have to go. Her family, her entire family was there. She had sisters and brothers, and you know, cousins and stuff. She had her entire family. And she wanted to go back to Vienna. And they all kept on saying, you have to come home. You have to come home. And then my father, I know he toyed with the idea of going to South America. I wish he would have done it. You could also go to Shanghai. That was a very remote possibility, because most people didn't want to go there. One of my uncles went there, I understand. I never heard from him though. He never wrote to me. I used to send money. That was the only thing. And he-- he toured. He came home. And he went-- went to Argentina and all these-- these places.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- but then, we heard from-- from the lad who said, you're crazy. We have-- you have two children. You don't know the language. They have to go to school. What are you going to do in a country like that? You don't know the language. You can't do anything if you don't know the language. So, he was persuaded that-- not only this, you have your family there. We'll help you. We do this, that, and the other thing. And so, he decided-- he decided, well, maybe at first we should go to Vienna for a short time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I was out of school. I had no place to go. So they decided they were going to ship me to me Vienna to my Uncle Arthur-- alone-- but until they could come-- until he could do something about his-- I never found out what happened to the stores-- never. Nobody ever told me. Children weren't told anything. I should have been told. I asked, but I was told don't worry about it. We'll take care of it, or something like that, you know. That's not your-- your thing to find out or.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your life like in Vienna with your uncle?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right, I went on-- on a-- on a train by myself. And my uncle and my cousin picked me up at the station in Vienna. And they took me to their house, which is also-- they had a large, as I said, he had a large apartment. Oh yeah, before I left, my mother took me aside to tell me the biggest secret. My uncle Arthur and his wife had been divorced. They were divorced. I mean divorce was something not very nice. Nice people didn't get divorced. I never found out what-- never. I tried very hard to find out. Nobody would tell me. I was so curious. But I was told that they lived in the same house, even though separate. Because, as I said, women didn't live alone in those days. And she had no-- she had only a child that was my age, a son. She had no children to go to, so she had no-- nobody to live with. So he gave her another-- a room, and a-- you know. She had her own room-- sitting bedroom in the house. And they didn't eat together, didn't go out together. They were just living like strangers in the same house. Even though she tried very hard to probably come back to-- I don't know whether she had a boyfriend. I know at that point she had a boyfriend. She used to get dressed and get-- go out. But I don't know. I never saw him or heard of him, or anything like that. But when I came to the-- the apartment, there was the housekeeper. Her name was Mitzi. And my cousin's name was Kurt-- K-U-R-T. He was exactly my age. He was just a couple of months difference. So, I thought he was very handsome and nice. And I have a picture of him. And they gave me a little nook in the-- in a large living room. There was a couch, and I was to sleep there, you know. And I was part of the household. And Mitzi took care of the house. She did the cooking, the shopping, the cleaning, everything. She even cleaned the-- the two rooms that he had as a dentist. But then Selma, was my aunt's name-- his ex-wife's name, Selma-- S-E-L-M-A-- Selma. Selma it was called in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was-- I thought she was very strange. At one point, I was sitting and talking to her. And all of a sudden, she went into a fit. And she wound up on the floor, and she had foam in her mouth. And I called Mitzi. I said, what's the matter? What?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, oh. She-- she came, and she held her hand, and she calmed her down. She says she has-- she's a epileptic. I never heard of that before. And she doesn't know when she's going to have a fit. So, she always has to take her medicine. She has to walk around with her medicine. And if this happens, don't worry about it. It's-- it's harmless. But at that time, it was not curable. I think today, they have stuff that-- you at least can stop these. But she never knew when she's going to have one of those things. So, that was another thing she had. And to me, she was-- I admired her. She had beautiful clothes. And she was the only woman I knew who wore make-up. Make-up was not used by nice girls-- only by not so nice girls-- [LAUGHS] --if you know what I mean. But it was not allowed. I remember when I got a little bigger, and I bought a lipstick. And I used to go to the theatre and put it on there. Because my father once saw me with a little-- with a little powder on my face, and he made me wash my face. That's how strict it was. But she wore all kinds of makeup, and I loved to see her put her makeup on. I learned how to put makeup on-- [LAUGHS] --just watching her. She tried to be nice to me, you know. But it was a very strange arrangement. And I used to go to Mitzi. Mitzi was the one that was with them from before Kurt was born-- that's how long-- maybe 20, 25 years. And she had brought him up. And he was her confidant. If he needed something, he went to Mitzi. He didn't go to his mother. They didn't get along at all. I don't know why. I never found out. I tried to find out. I tried to ask. He wouldn't talk about it.
